docs.title: django-planet
docs.text:
A reusable Django app for building RSS/Atom feed aggregator websites (aka "Planet" sites).

Django-planet makes it easy to create a planet-style feed aggregator. Collect posts from multiple blogs and websites, store them in your database, and display them with built-in views and templates — or build your own custom front-end.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• RSS and Atom feed parsing — Supports both RSS and Atom feed formats via feedparser
  • Automatic feed updates — Management commands to add feeds and update all feeds
  • Blog, Feed, Post, and Author models — Complete data model with relationships
  • Built-in views and templates — Ready-to-use views for blogs, feeds, posts, and authors
  • Django admin integration — Manage all content through Django's admin interface, including an "Add Feed by URL" workflow
  • Search functionality — Built-in search across posts, blogs, feeds, and authors
  • SEO-friendly URLs — Slugified URLs with automatic redirects
  • Custom managers — Chainable QuerySet methods for filtering by blog, feed, author
  • Template tags — Custom template tags and filters for common operations
  • Pagination support — Uses django-pagination-py3 for easy pagination
  • Post filtering — Configurable filter backends to accept only relevant posts
  • Content archiving — Optionally fetch and store the full original content of posts

docs.title: Quick Install
docs.text:
pip install django-planet

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ...
    "planet",
    "pagination",
]

MIDDLEWARE = [
    # ...
    "pagination.middleware.PaginationMiddleware",
]

python manage.py migrate

See the Installation guide for full setup instructions.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation & Configuration — Full setup guide
  • Usage — Adding feeds, updating, views, and search
  • Configuration Reference — All settings, filter backends, and logging
  • Demo & Screenshots — Live demo and example project

docs.title: Admin Interface
docs.text: Django-planet registers all models in the Django admin with search, filtering, and cross-linking between related objects.

docs.title: "Add Feed by URL" Workflow
docs.text:
The Feed admin has a special workflow for adding new feeds. When you click "Add Feed", you only need to provide the feed URL:

  1. Enter the feed URL and click Save
  2. django-planet automatically creates a Blog entry (using the feed's domain as a placeholder title) if one doesn't already exist
  3. A Feed stub is created, ready to be populated on the next planet_update_all_feeds run

This is the same operation as python manage.py planet_add_feed <url>, but accessible from the admin.

Note

The feed's title and metadata will be populated automatically the next time feeds are updated.

docs.title: BlogAdmin
docs.text:
• List display: title, URL, date created
  • Search: by title, URL
  • Inline feeds: Read-only tabular inline showing all feeds for the blog, with links to each feed's admin page

docs.title: FeedAdmin
docs.text:
• List display: title, URL, blog, language, etag, last modified, last checked, active status
  • List filter: by language
  • Search: by title, URL, blog title
  • Fieldsets:
    • General: title, URL, blog, language
    • Feed Status: etag, last modified, last checked, is_active
    • Authors: read-only list of all authors who have posts in this feed, with links to each author's admin page

docs.title: PostAdmin
docs.text:
• List display: title, feed, guid, date published, date created
  • List filter: by feed title, language
  • Search: by title, blog title
  • Optimized queries: uses select_related for feed and blog to minimize database queries

docs.title: AuthorAdmin
docs.text:
• List display: name, email
  • Search: by name
  • Fieldsets:
    • General: name, email, profile URL
    • Feeds: read-only list of all feeds this author has contributed to, with links to each feed's admin page

docs.title: PostAuthorDataAdmin
docs.text:
• List display: author name, is_contributor flag, post
  • List filter: by is_contributor, author

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: All django-planet settings are defined in a single PLANET dictionary in your Django settings module. Any key you don't specify falls back to its default.

docs.title: PLANET_CONFIG
docs.text:
PLANET = {
    "USER_AGENT": "MyPlanet/1.0",
    "RECENT_POSTS_LIMIT": 10,
    "RECENT_BLOGS_LIMIT": 10,
    "POST_FILTER_BACKEND": "planet.backends.accept_all.AcceptAllBackend",
    "TOPIC_KEYWORDS": [],
    "FETCH_ORIGINAL_CONTENT": False,
    "FETCH_CONTENT_DELAY": 0,
}

docs.title: Settings Reference
docs.text:
USER_AGENT

Type: str Default: "Django Planet/<version>"

The User-Agent header sent when fetching feeds and post content.

RECENT_POSTS_LIMIT

Type: int Default: 10

Number of posts shown by the {% recent_posts %} template tag.

RECENT_BLOGS_LIMIT

Type: int Default: 10

Number of blogs shown by the {% recent_blogs %} template tag.

POST_FILTER_BACKEND

Type: str (dotted Python path) Default: "planet.backends.accept_all.AcceptAllBackend"

The backend class used to filter incoming feed entries before they are saved. See Post Filter Backends below.

TOPIC_KEYWORDS

Type: list[str] Default: []

Keywords used by KeywordFilterBackend to filter posts. Only entries whose title or summary contains at least one keyword (case-insensitive) are accepted.

FETCH_ORIGINAL_CONTENT

Type: bool Default: False

When True, django-planet fetches the full HTML of each post's original URL using readability-lxml and stores it in Post.original_content. See Usage > Content Archiving.

FETCH_CONTENT_DELAY

Type: int | float Default: 0

Seconds to wait between content fetches. Set this to a positive value (e.g., 1) to be polite to origin servers.

docs.title: Post Filter Backends
docs.text: By default, all feed entries are saved. You can configure a post filter backend to accept only relevant posts before they are stored.

docs.title: AcceptAllBackend (default)
docs.text:
PLANET = {
    "POST_FILTER_BACKEND": "planet.backends.accept_all.AcceptAllBackend",
}

Accepts every entry unchanged. No configuration required.

docs.title: KeywordFilterBackend
docs.text:
PLANET = {
    "POST_FILTER_BACKEND": "planet.backends.keyword.KeywordFilterBackend",
    "TOPIC_KEYWORDS": ["python", "django", "open source"],
}

Accepts entries whose title or summary contains at least one of the configured keywords (case-insensitive). Rejected entries are logged at INFO level.

When TOPIC_KEYWORDS is empty, the backend accepts all entries (fail-open).

docs.title: Writing a Custom Backend
docs.text:
Subclass BasePostFilterBackend and implement filter_entries:

from planet.backends.base import BasePostFilterBackend


class MyBackend(BasePostFilterBackend):
    def filter_entries(self, entries, feed):
        # entries: list of feedparser entry objects
        # feed: planet.models.Feed instance
        return [e for e in entries if passes_my_check(e)]

Then point to it in your settings:

PLANET = {
    "POST_FILTER_BACKEND": "myapp.backends.MyBackend",
}

docs.title: Logging
docs.text:
django-planet uses Python's standard logging module. All loggers use names under the planet.* namespace (e.g., planet.utils, planet.management.commands.planet_update_all_feeds).

Following Python library best practices, no handlers are attached by default — the host project controls all logging output. Add a LOGGING configuration to see log output:

LOGGING = {
    "version": 1,
    "disable_existing_loggers": False,
    "handlers": {
        "console": {
            "class": "logging.StreamHandler",
        },
    },
    "loggers": {
        "planet": {
            "handlers": ["console"],
            "level": "INFO",  # Use "DEBUG" for more verbosity
        },
    },
}

At INFO level you'll see feed add/update summaries and 304 skips. At DEBUG level you'll also see individual fetch details, per-entry creation, and to_datetime() edge cases.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Contributions are welcome!

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
1. Fork and clone the repository:

    git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/django-planet.git
cd django-planet

  2. Install development dependencies:

    pip install -e .
pip install pre-commit

  3. Set up pre-commit hooks:

    pre-commit install

  4. Verify everything works:

    pre-commit run --all-files
hatch run test:test

docs.title: Repository Layout
docs.text:
planet/          # The reusable Django app (models, views, urls, admin, templatetags, management commands)
project/         # Example Django project for local development
tests/           # Test suite using Django's test runner + factory_boy
docs/            # MkDocs documentation (this site)

docs.title: Models
docs.text: Five models: Blog -> Feed -> Post <-M2M-> Author (via PostAuthorData junction table). Each model has a custom manager in planet/managers.py with chainable QuerySet methods. See Models & Managers for details.

docs.title: Views
docs.text: Standard list/detail views for blogs, feeds, posts, and authors, plus a search dispatcher. All use django-pagination-py3 for pagination. See Usage > Built-in Views.

docs.title: Feed Parsing
docs.text: parse_feed() in planet/utils.py uses feedparser to fetch and parse RSS/Atom feeds, with etag/last_modified support for efficient conditional requests.

docs.title: Management Commands
docs.text:
• planet_add_feed <url> — adds a feed (auto-creates blog)
  • planet_update_all_feeds — fetches new posts from all active feeds
  • planet_fetch_post_content — backfills original post content

docs.title: With Hatch (recommended)
docs.text:
# Run full test suite across all Python/Django combinations
hatch run test:test

# Run with coverage report
hatch run test:cov

# Run for a specific Python/Django combination
hatch run test.py3.12-5.2:test

# View all available test environments
hatch env show test

docs.title: Without Hatch
docs.text:
# Install test dependencies
pip install coverage factory_boy

# Run tests
python -m django test --settings tests.settings

# Run a specific test
python -m django test tests.test_views.BlogViewsTest --settings tests.settings

# Run with coverage
coverage run -m django test --settings tests.settings
coverage report

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text:
• Line length: 120 characters
  • Formatting: ruff + black (enforced by pre-commit hooks)
  • Linting: ruff, codespell, pyupgrade
  • Type checking: hatch run default:mypy planet project tests

docs.title: After Any Change
docs.text:
After making any change to the codebase, always do these three things in order:

  1. Run pre-commit hooks (formatting, linting):

    pre-commit run -a

  2. Run the full test suite:

    hatch run test:test

  3. Regenerate translation files:

    cd planet/ && hatch run django-admin makemessages -l es --settings=tests.settings

docs.title: Translation Workflow
docs.text:
django-planet supports internationalization. To update or generate translation files, you must run makemessages from inside the planet/ directory:

cd planet/
hatch run django-admin makemessages -l es --settings=tests.settings

docs.title: Pull Request Workflow
docs.text:
1. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/my-feature
  2. Make your changes
  3. Follow the After Any Change checklist
  4. Commit your changes: git commit -m "Add my feature"
  5. Push to your fork: git push origin feature/my-feature
  6. Open a Pull Request against main

docs.title: Live Demo
docs.text: A live instance of django-planet is running at django-planet.matagus.dev.

docs.title: Example Project
docs.text: The repository includes a fully configured example project in the project/ directory. It uses the same setup as the live demo.

docs.title: Running Locally
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/matagus/django-planet.git
cd django-planet

# Run migrations
hatch run project:migrate

# Start the development server
hatch run project:runserver

Then open http://localhost:8000 in your browser.

You can add feeds via the admin or the management command:

hatch run project:shell
# or
python manage.py planet_add_feed https://example.com/feed.xml --settings project.project.settings

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+
  • Django 4.0+

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pipFrom source 
pip install django-planet

git clone https://github.com/matagus/django-planet.git
cd django-planet
pip install -e .

docs.title: 1. Add to INSTALLED_APPS
docs.text:
Add planet and pagination to your INSTALLED_APPS in settings.py:

INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # Django apps
    "django.contrib.admin",
    "django.contrib.auth",
    "django.contrib.contenttypes",
    "django.contrib.sessions",
    "django.contrib.messages",
    "django.contrib.staticfiles",
    "django.contrib.sites",

    # Third-party apps
    "planet",
    "pagination",  # Required dependency
]

django.contrib.sites required

django-planet uses Django's sites framework. Make sure django.contrib.sites is in your INSTALLED_APPS and that you have SITE_ID = 1 (or the appropriate site ID) in your settings.

docs.title: 2. Add Pagination Middleware
docs.text:
MIDDLEWARE = [
    # ... other middleware
    "pagination.middleware.PaginationMiddleware",
]

docs.title: 3. Add Context Processor
docs.text:
Add the planet context processor to make site, SITE_NAME, search_form, and PLANET_CONFIG available in all templates:

TEMPLATES = [
    {
        "BACKEND": "django.template.backends.django.DjangoTemplates",
        "DIRS": [],
        "APP_DIRS": True,
        "OPTIONS": {
            "context_processors": [
                # ... default context processors
                "planet.context_processors.context",
            ],
        },
    },
]

docs.title: 4. Include URL Configuration
docs.text:
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    path("admin/", admin.site.urls),
    path("", include("planet.urls")),  # or path("planet/", include("planet.urls"))
]

docs.title: 5. Run Migrations
docs.text: python manage.py migrate

docs.title: 6. Optional Settings
docs.text:
You can customize django-planet's behavior via the PLANET dict in your settings:

PLANET = {
    "USER_AGENT": "MyPlanet/1.0",
    "RECENT_POSTS_LIMIT": 10,
    "RECENT_BLOGS_LIMIT": 10,
}

See the Configuration Reference for all available settings, including post filter backends and content archiving.

docs.title: Model Relationships
docs.text:
django-planet uses five models with the following relationships:

Blog → Feed → Post ←M2M→ Author
                ↕
          PostAuthorData (junction table)
  • A Blog has one or more Feeds
  • A Feed belongs to one Blog and contains many Posts
  • A Post belongs to one Feed and has many Authors (via PostAuthorData)
  • An Author can have posts across multiple feeds and blogs

docs.title: Blog
docs.text:
Represents a blog or website whose feeds are aggregated.

Key fields: title, url (unique), date_created

docs.title: Feed
docs.text:
Stores detailed info about a parsed Atom or RSS feed.

Key fields: blog (FK), url (unique), title, subtitle, language, guid (unique, MD5 hash), etag, last_modified, last_checked, is_active

docs.title: Post
docs.text:
A single entry from a feed.

Key fields: feed (FK), title, url, guid (unique, MD5 hash), content, original_content (nullable — populated when content archiving is enabled), language, date_published, date_modified, date_created

docs.title: Author
docs.text:
An author or contributor of posts.

Key fields: name, email, profile_url

docs.title: PostAuthorData
docs.text: Junction table linking posts to authors, with an is_contributor flag to distinguish original authors from contributors.

docs.title: Custom Managers
docs.text: Each model has a custom manager with chainable QuerySet methods.

docs.title: BlogManager
docs.text:
• published() — Blogs that have at least one checked feed
  • for_author(author) — Blogs with posts by the given author
  • search(query) — Filter by title (case-insensitive contains)

docs.title: FeedManager
docs.text:
• active() — Feeds where is_active=True
  • published() — Feeds that have been checked at least once
  • for_author(author) — Feeds with posts by the given author
  • search(query) — Filter by title (case-insensitive contains)

docs.title: PostManager
docs.text:
• for_blog(blog) — Posts from any feed belonging to the given blog
  • for_feed(feed) — Posts from the given feed
  • for_author(author) — Posts by the given author
  • search(query) — Filter by title (case-insensitive contains)
  • with_relations() — Select/prefetch related Feed, Blog, and Authors

docs.title: AuthorManager
docs.text:
• for_blog(blog) — Authors who have posts in feeds belonging to the given blog
  • search(query) — Filter by name (case-insensitive contains)

All QuerySet methods are chainable:

Post.objects.for_blog(blog).search("django").with_relations()

For full field definitions and implementation details, see the source code.

docs.title: Template Directory
docs.text:
Django-planet includes a complete set of templates:

planet/templates/planet/
├── base.html
├── posts/
│   ├── list.html
│   ├── detail.html
│   └── blocks/
│       └── list.html
├── blogs/
│   ├── list.html
│   ├── detail.html
│   └── blocks/
│       └── list.html
├── feeds/
│   ├── list.html
│   ├── detail.html
│   └── blocks/
│       └── list_for_author.html
└── authors/
    ├── list.html
    ├── detail.html
    └── blocks/
        ├── list.html
        └── list_for_feed.html

docs.title: Overriding Templates
docs.text:
Django-planet templates can be overridden using Django's standard template loading mechanism. Place your custom templates in your project's template directory, matching the same path structure:

your_project/
└── templates/
    └── planet/
        └── posts/
            └── list.html    # Overrides planet's default post list

Make sure your project's template directory is listed before APP_DIRS in your TEMPLATES setting, or use DIRS to specify it explicitly.

docs.title: Template Tags Reference
docs.text:
Load the template tags in any template:

{% load planet_tags %}

docs.title: clean_html
docs.text:
Cleans HTML content by extracting the readable article body using readability-lxml. Removes clutter like navigation, ads, and extraneous markup, returning just the main content.

{{ post.content|clean_html }}

Returns safe HTML (won't be escaped).

docs.title: get_first_paragraph
docs.text:
Extracts the first paragraph or sentence from post content. Useful for creating excerpts.

{% get_first_paragraph post.content as excerpt %}
<p>{{ excerpt }}</p>

How it works:

  • Strips all HTML tags
  • Normalizes whitespace
  • Returns the first sentence longer than 80 characters
  • Falls back to the first 80 characters if no long sentence is found

docs.title: get_authors_for_blog
docs.text:
Returns all authors who have written posts for a specific blog.

{% get_authors_for_blog blog as authors %}
{% for author in authors %}
    <a href="{{ author.get_absolute_url }}">{{ author.name }}</a>
{% endfor %}

docs.title: blogs_for_author
docs.text:
Returns all blogs that an author has contributed to.

{% blogs_for_author author as blogs %}
{% for blog in blogs %}
    <a href="{{ blog.get_absolute_url }}">{{ blog.title }}</a>
{% endfor %}

docs.title: post_titles
docs.text:
Returns a comma-separated string of post titles from a queryset.

{% post_titles posts limit=5 as titles %}
<p>{{ titles }}</p>
Argument Default Description posts (required) A Post queryset limit 5 Maximum number of titles to include

docs.title: Inclusion Tags
docs.text: Inclusion tags render complete HTML blocks using their own templates.

docs.title: authors_for_feed
docs.text:
Renders a list of all authors who have posts in a feed.

{% authors_for_feed feed %}

Template: planet/authors/blocks/list_for_feed.html

docs.title: feeds_for_author
docs.text:
Renders a list of all feeds an author has contributed to.

{% feeds_for_author author %}

Template: planet/feeds/blocks/list_for_author.html

docs.title: recent_posts
docs.text:
Renders a list of the most recent posts across all blogs.

{% recent_posts %}

Template: planet/posts/blocks/list.html

Limit controlled by PLANET["RECENT_POSTS_LIMIT"] (default: 10).

docs.title: recent_blogs
docs.text:
Renders a list of the most recently added blogs.

{% recent_blogs %}

Template: planet/blogs/blocks/list.html

Limit controlled by PLANET["RECENT_BLOGS_LIMIT"] (default: 10).

docs.title: Context Processor
docs.text:
The planet.context_processors.context processor makes the following variables available in all templates:

Variable Description site The current Site object (from django.contrib.sites) SITE_NAME The site's name (site.name) search_form A SearchForm instance (pre-filled if search params are in the GET request) PLANET_CONFIG The full planet configuration dictionary

Add it to your template context processors — see Installation.

docs.title: Adding Feeds
docs.text:
Add a feed using the management command:

python manage.py planet_add_feed https://example.com/feed.xml

This will:

  • Parse the feed and create a Blog entry if one doesn't already exist
  • Create the Feed entry
  • Import all posts from the feed
  • Create Author entries and link them to posts

You can also add feeds through the Django admin interface — see Admin Interface for details on the "Add Feed by URL" workflow.

docs.title: Updating Feeds
docs.text:
Fetch new posts from all active feeds:

python manage.py planet_update_all_feeds

This command:

  • Iterates through all active feeds
  • Uses etag and last_modified headers for efficient conditional requests (304 Not Modified)
  • Creates new Post and Author entries as needed
  • Updates feed metadata (last_checked, etag)

docs.title: Periodic Updates
docs.text:
For production, schedule planet_update_all_feeds to run periodically:

CronCelerysystemd timer 
# Run every hour
0 * * * * /path/to/venv/bin/python /path/to/project/manage.py planet_update_all_feeds

# celery.py or tasks.py
from celery import shared_task
from django.core.management import call_command

@shared_task
def update_feeds():
    call_command("planet_update_all_feeds")

# In your Celery beat schedule:
CELERY_BEAT_SCHEDULE = {
    "update-feeds": {
        "task": "yourapp.tasks.update_feeds",
        "schedule": 3600,  # every hour
    },
}

Create /etc/systemd/system/planet-update.service:

[Unit]
Description=Update django-planet feeds

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/path/to/venv/bin/python /path/to/project/manage.py planet_update_all_feeds
User=www-data

Create /etc/systemd/system/planet-update.timer:

[Unit]
Description=Run planet feed updates hourly

[Timer]
OnCalendar=hourly
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target

sudo systemctl enable --now planet-update.timer

docs.title: Original Content Archiving
docs.text: By default, django-planet stores only the feed summary for each post. You can optionally fetch and archive the full original content from each post's URL.

docs.title: Enable Archiving
docs.text:
PLANET = {
    "FETCH_ORIGINAL_CONTENT": True,
    "FETCH_CONTENT_DELAY": 1,  # seconds between fetches (be polite to servers)
}

When enabled, new posts will have their full content fetched automatically using readability-lxml to extract the article body. The result is stored in Post.original_content and displayed on the post detail page instead of the feed summary.

If fetching fails for a post, a WARNING is logged and original_content remains None — the feed summary is shown as fallback.

docs.title: Backfill Existing Posts
docs.text:
Use the planet_fetch_post_content command to backfill posts that were imported before archiving was enabled:

# Backfill all posts missing original_content
python manage.py planet_fetch_post_content

# Limit to a specific feed
python manage.py planet_fetch_post_content --feed 42

# Cap the number of posts processed
python manage.py planet_fetch_post_content --limit 100

docs.title: planet_add_feed
docs.text:
python manage.py planet_add_feed <feed_url>

Adds a new feed to the database. Creates the Blog (if it doesn't exist), Feed, Posts, and Authors.

docs.title: planet_update_all_feeds
docs.text:
python manage.py planet_update_all_feeds

Updates all active feeds. Fetches new posts, updates feed metadata (etag, last_checked), and creates new Post and Author entries as needed.

docs.title: planet_fetch_post_content
docs.text:
python manage.py planet_fetch_post_content [--feed <id>] [--limit <n>]

Backfills original_content for posts where it is missing.

Argument Description --feed <id> Limit to posts from a specific feed (by feed ID) --limit <n> Cap the number of posts processed

Respects PLANET["FETCH_CONTENT_DELAY"] between requests.

docs.title: Built-in Views
docs.text:
Django-planet provides a complete set of views with SEO-friendly URLs. If a request is missing the slug portion, it redirects permanently (301) to the canonical URL.

URL Pattern View Name Template Description / planet:index planet/posts/list.html Post list (index page) /posts/ planet:post-list planet/posts/list.html All posts /posts/<id>/<slug>/ planet:post-detail planet/posts/detail.html Post detail /blogs/ planet:blog-list planet/blogs/list.html All blogs /blogs/<id>/<slug>/ planet:blog-detail planet/blogs/detail.html Blog detail (all posts from that blog) /feeds/ planet:feed-list planet/feeds/list.html All feeds /feeds/<id>/<slug>/ planet:feed-detail planet/feeds/detail.html Feed detail (all posts from that feed) /authors/ planet:author-list planet/authors/list.html All authors /authors/<id>/<slug>/ planet:author-detail planet/authors/detail.html Author detail (all posts by that author) /search/ planet:search (dispatches to list views) Search endpoint

docs.title: Search
docs.text:
The search endpoint (/search/) accepts GET parameters and dispatches to the appropriate list view:

Parameter Description q The search query string w What to search: posts, blogs, feeds, or authors

Example: /search/?q=python&w=posts searches posts for "python".

The SearchForm is automatically available in all templates via the context processor as search_form.
